critic_raw:
Churn model review — single pass:

The evaluation's main weakness is using global ROC-AUC to justify a capacity-constrained workflow decision. The retention team will call exactly the top 2% of scored users each week. The relevant question is: does the new model produce a better top-2% list? AUC cannot answer this because it aggregates performance over all thresholds including the 98% of users who will never receive a retention call.

The missing metrics are: precision at the top-2% cutoff, recall at the top-2% cutoff, lift at the top-2% cutoff, and incremental retention lift among called users. These are all computable from the existing held-out month data (except perhaps incremental lift, which requires some experimental design).

The data science team's defense (AUC is threshold-independent and therefore more robust) misidentifies the problem. Threshold-independence is a limitation for this use case, not an advantage — the model is deployed at one threshold and the metric should reflect performance at that threshold.

Features (top-up behavior, complaint history, etc.) and monthly recalibration are reasonable. The absence of the right evaluation metric does not imply the model is bad — it means the evaluation has not demonstrated it is good for the specific purpose.

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed
adjudication_raw: Single-agent baseline run 2. All three must-find issues identified. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

empirical_test.measure: precision and lift at top-2% for both models on held-out test month
empirical_test.success_criterion: new model top-2% precision and lift exceed incumbent's, confirming operational improvement
empirical_test.failure_criterion: top-2% precision and lift are not improved for the new model, showing the AUC gain did not translate to the operational threshold
